name: Rohonc Codex
description: The Rohonc Codex is an illustrated manuscript of unknown origin, script, and language. It surfaced in Hungary in the early 19th century and has defied all attempts at decipherment. Proponents suggest it could be a key to a lost civilization, a secret religious text, or a hoax. The Codex consists of 448 paper pages, each featuring illustrations alongside text written in an unknown script. The illustrations depict religious, military, and everyday scenes. Hypotheses regarding its origin range from it being a Dacian text, a Sumero-Hungarian chronicle, a work of the Vlachs, or even a hoax created by Samuel Literati, the librarian to whom it was donated. Mainstream scholarship overwhelmingly considers the Codex undeciphered, with the majority of linguists and historians leaning towards the hoax hypothesis due to the lack of historical context, the seemingly arbitrary nature of the script, and the absence of corroborating evidence. The illustrations, while detailed, lack clear stylistic connections to any known historical art tradition, further fueling skepticism. The lack of provenance before its appearance in the 19th century also raises significant concerns about its authenticity and origin.
subtitle: Undeciphered manuscript of mysterious origin.

claims.claim: The Codex contains the history of a lost civilization.
claims.evidence: The complex illustrations and extensive text suggest a sophisticated narrative.
claims.criticism: The lack of any historical or archaeological evidence supporting the existence of such a civilization makes this claim highly speculative. The illustrations are stylistically ambiguous and do not match known historical art traditions.

claims.claim: The Codex is written in a secret language or code.
claims.evidence: The script is unlike any known writing system, and all attempts at decipherment have failed.
claims.criticism: The complexity of the script does not necessarily indicate a genuine language or code. It could be a deliberately obfuscated or invented system, as seen in known hoaxes.

claims.claim: The Codex is a genuine historical document of great importance.
claims.evidence: The sheer volume of text and illustrations suggests a significant investment of time and resources.
claims.criticism: The lack of provenance and the absence of corroborating evidence from other historical sources cast serious doubt on its authenticity. The cost of creating the Codex does not automatically imply historical significance.

keyFigures.name: Ferenc Kazinczy
keyFigures.role: First documented mention and failed attempt at decipherment.

keyFigures.name: Attila Türk
keyFigures.role: Proposed a connection to the Avar language.

keyFigures.name: Benedek Láng
keyFigures.role: Hungarian historian, skeptic, examines hoax theories.

mythologyConnections: Possible symbolism related to pagan beliefs (speculative)

timeline.event: Codex surfaces in the Batthyány Library in Rohonc (Rechnitz).
The library is donated to the Hungarian Academy of Sciences.
Ferenc Kazinczy attempts to decipher the Codex.
Continued attempts at decipherment and ongoing debate about its authenticity.

sources.author: Levente Zoltán Király
sources.work: The Rohonc Codex: A Cultural-Historical Reassessment
sources.title: Levente Zoltán Király
